title: Quiz: Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks
description: Test your understanding of QCNNs, quantum convolution, pooling, and hierarchical architectures

questions.question: What is the key property that makes QCNN convolutional layers parameter-efficient?

options.text: Using fewer qubits than classical bits
Translational invariance - same unitary applied at all positions

questions.explanation: Translational invariance means the same 2-qubit unitary is applied at every spatial position, reducing parameters from O(n) to O(1) per convolutional layer.

questions.question: How does quantum pooling typically reduce the number of qubits?

options.text: By removing qubits from the circuit
By measuring some qubits or tracing them out
By combining qubits into single qubits

questions.explanation: Quantum pooling reduces qubits by measuring every other qubit (with optional controlled operations) or by tracing out qubits, effectively halving the quantum register size.

questions.question: In a QCNN with n input qubits, how many pooling layers are typically needed to reach a single output qubit?
questions.explanation: Each pooling layer halves the number of qubits, so log2(n) layers are needed to go from n qubits to 1 qubit.

questions.question: QCNNs require the number of input qubits to be a power of 2 for clean hierarchical pooling.
questions.explanation: For standard hierarchical pooling that halves qubits at each layer, a power of 2 is required. Other sizes need padding or modified pooling strategies.

questions.question: How do QCNNs achieve nonlinearity compared to classical CNNs?

options.text: Through measurement collapse and quantum interference

questions.explanation: Unlike classical CNNs with explicit nonlinear activations, QCNNs get nonlinearity from quantum measurement (which collapses superpositions) and interference effects.

questions.question: What is measurement-based pooling in QCNNs?

options.text: Measuring all qubits at the output
Measuring some qubits and conditioning subsequent operations
Using classical pooling after quantum measurement

questions.explanation: Measurement-based pooling measures some qubits (typically every other one) and uses controlled operations conditioned on the measurement outcomes, effectively reducing the active qubit count.

questions.question: How does QCNN parameter count scale compared to classical CNN for input size n?

options.text: QCNN: O(log n), Classical: O(k^2 x filters x depth)

questions.explanation: QCNNs scale as O(log n) due to shared parameters and hierarchical structure, while classical CNNs scale with kernel size squared times number of filters times depth.

questions.question: Which are valid components of a QCNN architecture? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quantum convolutional layers with shared parameters
Quantum pooling layers that reduce qubit count
Hierarchical structure with progressive dimension reduction

questions.explanation: QCNNs consist of quantum convolutional layers (a), quantum pooling layers (b), and hierarchical structure (d). ReLU activations (c) are classical and not used in QCNNs.
